name: otlp
context: Configure the OpenTelemetry Protocol for traces, metrics, and logs over gRPC or HTTP.
info: OTLP is the standard OpenTelemetry wire protocol for exporting telemetry from SDKs to Collectors or backends. Agents use this node to set endpoints, choose transport, configure headers and TLS, understand default ports, and debug why an SDK sends to the wrong path. OTLP matters because it is the compatibility boundary: code can emit once and vendors can change behind the Collector or exporter configuration.
triggers: why is otlp adding v1 traces
body:
# OTLP

OTLP is the OpenTelemetry Protocol used by SDK exporters and Collectors. It supports traces, metrics, and logs. The two common transports are OTLP/gRPC on port `4317` and OTLP/HTTP on port `4318`.

## Endpoint Rules

```bash
# One HTTP endpoint for all signals. SDKs append signal paths.
export OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT=http://collector:4318
# traces -> http://collector:4318/v1/traces
# metrics -> http://collector:4318/v1/metrics
# logs -> http://collector:4318/v1/logs

# Signal-specific endpoint is used as-is.
export OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_TRACES_ENDPOINT=http://collector:4318/v1/traces
export OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_METRICS_ENDPOINT=https://metrics.example.com/v1/metrics
```

When using the general `OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT` with OTLP/HTTP, SDKs build per-signal paths. When using `OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_TRACES_ENDPOINT`, `OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_METRICS_ENDPOINT`, or `OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_LOGS_ENDPOINT`, provide the final URL yourself.

## Transport

```bash
export OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_PROTOCOL=http/protobuf
# Other common values: grpc, http/json
```

Use `grpc` when the SDK and network support HTTP/2 cleanly. Use `http/protobuf` when proxies, serverless platforms, or edge networks make gRPC harder. Use `http/json` mainly for debugging or constrained interoperability.

## Headers and Auth

```bash
export OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_HEADERS=authorization=Bearer%20$OTEL_TOKEN,x-tenant=my-team
export OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_TIMEOUT=10000
export OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_COMPRESSION=gzip
```

Header values are commonly URL-encoded in environment variables. Keep exporter tokens in secret stores, not source code or committed config.

## Collector Receiver Example

```yaml
receivers:
  otlp:
    protocols:
      grpc:
        endpoint: 0.0.0.0:4317
      http:
        endpoint: 0.0.0.0:4318
service:
  pipelines:
    traces:
      receivers: [otlp]
      processors: [batch]
      exporters: [debug]
```

sections.title: Ports and paths
sections.body: OTLP/gRPC commonly listens on `4317`. OTLP/HTTP commonly listens on `4318`. For the general HTTP endpoint env var, exporters append `/v1/traces`, `/v1/metrics`, or `/v1/logs`; for signal-specific endpoint env vars, the URL is used as-is. This distinction explains many 404s and empty collectors.

sections.title: Environment variables
sections.body: `OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT` sets the base endpoint. Signal-specific variants override it: `OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_TRACES_ENDPOINT`, `OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_METRICS_ENDPOINT`, and `OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_LOGS_ENDPOINT`. `OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_PROTOCOL` chooses transport. `OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_HEADERS` carries auth or tenancy headers. `OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_CERTIFICATE`, `OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_CLIENT_KEY`, and `OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_CLIENT_CERTIFICATE` support TLS and mTLS where SDKs implement them.

sections.title: Collector boundary
sections.body: Point applications at [[opentelemetry/collector]] rather than every vendor endpoint when you need fan-out, redaction, batching, retries, or policy control. The app usually exports OTLP to the nearest collector; the collector then exports OTLP, Prometheus remote write, debug logs, or vendor-specific formats.
